summary: Controls the SMB2 value for LanmanServer. Microsoft directly documents the same LanmanServer registry path, value name, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for disabling the SMBv2 and SMBv3 server protocols.

setting.name: SMBv2 and SMBv3 Server Protocol Support
setting.casual_explanation: SMBv2 and SMBv3 are the modern Windows file-sharing protocols. Turning them off forces the machine away from the normal modern SMB stack.
setting.why_people_change_it: People disable SMBv2 and SMBv3 only for special legacy troubleshooting or when testing very old environments. Modern Windows systems normally should keep them enabled.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling SMBv2 and SMBv3 is officially supported but very risky. Modern file sharing, performance features, encryption, signing improvements, and many Windows workflows can break.
setting.professional_notes: Microsoft documents SMB2 under HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters. A value of 0 disables SMBv2 and SMBv3 on the server component. If the value does not exist, the default is enabled.

allowed_values.label: Enabled by default
allowed_values.meaning: Windows keeps SMBv2 and SMBv3 enabled when no explicit override exists.

allowed_values.label: Disabled
allowed_values.meaning: The SMBv2 and SMBv3 server protocols are disabled.

allowed_values.label: Enabled
allowed_values.meaning: The SMBv2 and SMBv3 server protocols are enabled explicitly.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes SMB2 = 0, which matches Microsoft's documented disable behavior for SMBv2 and SMBv3 on the server component.

windows_defaults.label: Windows SMB server default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Supported Windows SMB server releases with SMBv2 and SMBv3 support

states.rationale: Microsoft documents that the default configuration is enabled and the registry value may not exist.

windows_defaults.notes: This is the normal modern Windows baseline.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
avoid_for: Special legacy troubleshooting where modern SMB must be disabled intentionally

states.rationale: Leave the explicit override absent and keep modern SMB enabled.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the normal Windows SMB stack and modern compatibility.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are intentionally testing an old legacy-only environment.
tradeoffs: You are not forcing a legacy-only server mode.

recommended_profiles.label: Legacy-only server

states.rationale: Disable SMBv2 and SMBv3 on the server component.

recommended_profiles.good_when: You have a specific and temporary reason to disable the modern SMB server protocols.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You are doing it as a general optimization or hardening step.
tradeoffs: Modern SMB behavior can break badly.
This is usually harmful outside very specific troubleshooting.

evidence.title: Microsoft Learn: detect, enable, and disable SMBv1, SMBv2, and SMBv3 in Windows
evidence.summary: Microsoft documents the LanmanServer SMB2 registry value, including 0 for disabled, 1 for enabled, and the default behavior where the value may not exist because SMBv2 and SMBv3 are enabled.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes SMB2 = 0 for the current tweak.

decision.why: Microsoft directly documents HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters -> SMB2, including 0 for Disabled, 1 for Enabled, and the default-enabled behavior when the value is absent. The app writes that same documented value on the documented server-side SMB path.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: To enable or disable SMBv2 on an SMB server, open Registry Editor and go to HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\LanmanServer\Parameters. For the name, use SMB2. For the type, use REG_DWORD. For the data, use 0 for Disabled and 1 for Enabled. The default value is 1, or Enabled. In this case, no registry key is created.
validation_proof.notes: Microsoft documents the same LanmanServer registry path, value name, and 0/1 semantics that the app uses for the server-side SMBv2 and SMBv3 disable override.
